md: Linear functions add a constant amount each step ($y = mx + b$). **Exponential functions** *multiply* by a constant amount each step. That tiny shift in rule produces wildly different behavior — exponentials describe everything from population growth to compound interest to radioactive decay.
Here $a$ is the **starting value** (when $x = 0$, $y = a \cdot b^0 = a$) and $b$ is the **base** — the multiplier per step. The behavior depends entirely on whether $b > 1$ or $b < 1$:

blocks.title: Growth vs decay
blocks.body:
**$b > 1$**: each step multiplies by something bigger than 1 → values grow. **Exponential growth.**
**$0 < b < 1$**: each step multiplies by a fraction → values shrink toward zero. **Exponential decay.**
**$b = 1$**: no change. The graph is a horizontal line at $y = a$.

md: Try the widget below. Set $b = 2$ for doubling growth, $b = 0.5$ for halving decay, $b = 1$ for no change. Watch how the curve hugs the x-axis on one end and shoots off on the other.
**Compound interest.** Money in an account that earns rate $r$ per period: $A = P(1 + r)^t$. Here $b = 1 + r > 1$ — growth.
**Population.** A population that grows by 3% per year: $P = P_0(1.03)^t$. Growth ($b = 1.03$).
**Half-life.** A radioactive sample halves every $T$ time units: $A = A_0(0.5)^{t/T}$. Decay ($b = 0.5$).
**Cooling.** A hot object cooling toward room temperature also follows an exponential decay law (Newton's law of cooling).

When the multiplier is *continuously compounding*, you get a special base: $e \approx 2.71828$. The function $y = e^x$ is **the** exponential function for calculus — its rate of change at every point equals its current value, which makes it pop up everywhere.

blocks.title: What makes exponential growth feel impossible
blocks.body: Linear growth is steady: $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, \ldots$. Exponential growth (doubling) starts slow and explodes: $1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, \ldots$. After $30$ doublings: over a billion. After $40$: over a trillion. This is why a virus, a viral video, or compound interest can blow past human intuition — the *long tail* of exponential growth is bigger than the entire history before it.

md: A bacterial population of 100 doubles every hour. How many bacteria after 5 hours?
Model: $P(t) = 100 \cdot 2^t$. After $5$ hours: $P(5) = 100 \cdot 2^5 = 100 \cdot 32 = 3200$. After $10$ hours: $100 \cdot 1024 \approx 100{,}000$. After $20$ hours: over $100$ million.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: $y = a \cdot b^x$. **$b > 1$**: growth. **$0 < b < 1$**: decay. **$a$**: starting value at $x = 0$. The base $e \approx 2.718$ is the natural exponential, the foundation of continuous compounding and most of calculus.
